visualizations.title: How a Model Gets Made
visualizations.description: Reality is simplified with assumptions into a model, which predicts things until it breaks down

Messy Reality
(too complex to calculate exactly)

Model Breaks Down

visualizations.title: The Point-Mass Model
visualizations.description: Newton's second law already assumes a model: treat the object as a single point

steps.explanation: This formula predicts how an object accelerates - but only after you've simplified it.
F = ma treats the whole object as a single point with no size or shape. That's the model. It ignores spin, wobble, and air resistance.
You need spin (a ball's curveball), or the object is too light and air resistance dominates (a feather). Then the point-mass model isn't good enough.

visualizations.title: Real Object vs. Its Model
visualizations.description: A detailed sphere (the real ball) shrinking down to a point (the model physicists actually calculate with)

visualizations.title: Real Ball vs. Point-Mass Model
visualizations.description: The real ball spins and wobbles as it falls; the point-mass model just falls straight down under gravity

visualizations.title: What the Point-Mass Model Predicts
visualizations.description: Height above the ground over time for an object dropped from 20 m, using the simple point-mass falling model

visualizations.title: Models Are Useful Lies
visualizations.description: A quick recap of why physics uses models

spec.body_markdown:
## Models in Physics

A model is a simplified version of reality. Not perfectly accurate, but accurate enough to be useful.

Some everyday examples:

- **Atoms as little balls** - not really true (they're quantum), but great for visualizing
- **Light as a ray (straight line)** - light is really a wave, but rays work fine for mirrors and lenses
- **A ball as a point** - ignore its size and shape, and F = ma still tells you exactly where it lands

The skill isn't memorizing models - it's knowing what each one gets right, what it ignores, and when it stops working.

visualizations.title: Model vs. Reality
visualizations.description: Toggle between the real object and its simplified point-mass model while it falls

controls.label: Show point-mass model instead of real object

visualizations.title: Common Models Physics Uses
